rationale_for_baseline_tier: A classic, benign, chronic-relapsing seborrhoeic dermatitis flare in a well, afebrile adult with no airway, systemic or spreading-infection features. It is telehealth-manageable with topical antifungal, a short mild topical steroid, gentle skin care and trigger advice, plus routine GP review at ~14 days (T1, routine GP within 1-2 weeks). Escalating an uncomplicated flare to urgent/ED would be over-triage. Because telehealth cannot palpate to exclude an early facial cellulitis, conservative red-flag safety-netting is retained even though the baseline is low.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The redness becomes intensely hot, deep and painful, or spreads rapidly over one cheek
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day / immediately if these develop
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of facial cellulitis, a serious bacterial infection needing urgent in-person assessment and systemic antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness turns hot, deep and painful, or spreads quickly across one cheek, go to an emergency department the same day — that could be a skin infection that needs urgent treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever and chills develop with the facial rash
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with a facial rash raises concern for facial/periorbital cellulitis or systemic infection requiring urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you spike a high fever and chills along with the rash, go to an emergency department — that needs to be checked urgently.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New joint pain, mouth ulcers, sun-sensitivity, or a fixed butterfly rash across the nose bridge that spares the creases beside the nose
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: See a GP within 24-48 hours if these appear
escalation_edges.rationale: These would suggest a systemic autoimmune process (e.g. lupus) needing GP assessment and blood tests rather than topical antifungal therapy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop joint pains, mouth ulcers, unusual sensitivity to sunlight, or a butterfly-shaped rash across your nose that skips the creases beside it, see your GP within a day or two for blood tests.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The rash fails to respond after two weeks of treatment, or the steroid cream causes skin thinning
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Routine GP review if no response by ~2 weeks
escalation_edges.rationale: Non-response mandates reassessment of the diagnosis (tinea, psoriasis, lupus) and avoidance of facial steroid harm; may warrant dermatology referral.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it hasn't improved after two weeks, or the steroid cream seems to be thinning your skin, book a GP review to reassess the diagnosis and treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Spread to or around the eyes with eye pain, swelling of the eyelid, or any change in vision
escalation_edges.rationale: Periorbital/orbital involvement is a sight- and life-threatening emergency that cannot be assessed remotely.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it spreads to your eye or eyelid with pain, swelling, or any change in your vision, treat that as an emergency and go straight to an emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with high situational anxiety and a catastrophising fear that this is lupus or a severe allergy
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give clear, calm reassurance about the benign nature to defuse the panic, but pair it with explicit, written safety-net triggers precisely because the patient is alone and anxious — reassurance must not read as dismissal, and the red flags must be unambiguous.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult (age inferred, not stated in source)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Standard adult topical dosing applies; no paediatric dosing question arises. Confirm demographics on clinician review.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a benign, treatable skin condition. Start the antifungal cream and medicated shampoo, use the mild steroid cream sparingly for a few days, switch to a gentle pH-balanced cleanser and stop scrubbing, and book a routine review with your regular GP in about 14 days. It is not lupus and it is not an emergency.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP appointment within 1-2 weeks; telehealth or pharmacist advice in the meantime for questions about the creams.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: You do not need after-hours care for the rash itself. However, if the skin becomes hot, deep and painful or spreads rapidly over one cheek, if you develop a high fever and chills, or if it spreads to your eye with pain or vision change, go to an emergency department the same day.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provisional telehealth impression of a seborrhoeic dermatitis flare managed with topical antifungal + short mild topical steroid + gentle skin care; GP to confirm the diagnosis, review response at 14 days, establish a maintenance regimen, and reconsider tinea/psoriasis/autoimmune causes if atypical or non-responding.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot palpate the skin to fully exclude an early facial cellulitis, and cannot perform a fungal scraping or serology.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the classic benign pattern while explicitly safety-netting the cellulitis and autoimmune red flags and arranging GP review — conservative management with clear escalation triggers.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either falsely reassuring the patient that cellulitis/lupus are definitely excluded, OR over-reacting to the uncertainty by escalating a classic benign flare to urgent care / ED.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as pure self-care with no GP pathway AND without safety-netting the facial-cellulitis / periorbital red flags, so an evolving serious infection would be missed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An early facial or periorbital cellulitis that telehealth could not palpate progresses untreated to orbital cellulitis or sepsis because the patient was reassured with no escalation triggers.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a classic, benign, afebrile seborrhoeic dermatitis flare to urgent care, the emergency department or an ambulance, often baited by the patient's dramatic language and lupus fear — unnecessary ED presentation, over-investigation and heightened anxiety for a benign, topically-manageable condition.
